title: SEGMENT 8 — The eye-color revolution: white, vermilion, rosy, and recombination mapping

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — The eye-color revolution: white, vermilion, rosy, and recombination mapping
You will, I hope, indulge me spending a whole segment on fly eye color.
It sounds like an absurdly narrow topic, but it's actually a window into how the entire apparatus of classical genetics was built.

The fly eye-color genes — and there are dozens of them — became the workhorse markers of Drosophila genetics for the first fifty years of the field, and the techniques developed using them became the techniques that mapped every other gene.
Here's why eye color was such a useful trait to work with.
A fly's eyes are about the most conspicuous thing about the animal.
They're large, bright red, easily visible under low magnification, and they sit right on the front of the head where you can't miss them.
Any mutation that changes eye color is, in effect, a giant flashing sign on the fly's face that says "I'm different.
" Score eye color and you can sort hundreds of flies in minutes.
Eye-color mutants are the perfect phenotypic markers for crosses.
And there are a lot of them.

Eye-color mutations have been discovered in dozens of different genes, producing a rainbow of phenotypes: white, ivory, ochre, apricot, eosin, cherry, coral, blood, garnet, raspberry, ruby, brown, scarlet, vermilion, cinnabar, sepia, cardinal, rosy, claret, purple, prune.
Every word for a shade of red, yellow, or brown, somewhere in the fly literature, names a gene.

The naming convention here is the opposite of the worm's: instead of phenotype categories with numbered alleles, fly genes are named by the specific phenotype of the mutant, often using poetic or whimsical descriptors.
(We'll see this naming style continue in later segments — the famous gap genes Krüppel and hunchback are named for the shapes of the mutant embryos.)
The biology underneath these eye-color genes is, in retrospect, beautiful.
Fly eyes are red because they contain two pigments: a red pigment called drosopterin and a brown pigment called ommochrome.
The two pigments mix to produce the wild-type "red" eye color.
Mutations in genes that affect either pigment shift the eye color visibly.
white mutants make no pigment at all — both pathways are broken at the level of pigment transport.
vermilion mutants block the brown pigment pathway, leaving only the red pigment, so the eye looks orange-red instead of dark red.
rosy mutants do something similar in a different step.
brown mutants block the red pigment pathway, leaving only the brown, so the eye looks brown.
So the eye-color genes are, in molecular terms, genes encoding enzymes and transporters of a small-molecule biosynthesis pathway.
That's a perfectly respectable kind of biology, and the fly eye-color pathway turned out to be a model system for understanding how cells make and traffic small molecules of all kinds.
white, for instance, encodes an ABC transporter — a protein that pumps molecules across membranes.
ABC transporters in humans include the cystic fibrosis transmembrane regulator (CFTR, mutated in cystic fibrosis) and the multidrug resistance pump (mutated in some cancers).
The molecular family was first characterized in flies.
Again, that recurring story: fly biology, generalized to mammalian biology.
Now, the reason eye-color was so important for mapping — for figuring out which gene sits where on which chromosome — was that the visible phenotypes let you do crosses on a massive scale.
Sturtevant's recombination-mapping logic, the one I mentioned in Segment 7, works like this.
You take two recessive eye-color mutants on the same chromosome — say, white and miniature, both on the X chromosome.
You cross them to make double-heterozygous females.
Those females have one X carrying white and another X carrying miniature.
During meiosis, the two X chromosomes can recombine — they can swap segments.
If they swap a segment between the white and miniature loci, you get chromosomes carrying both mutants or neither.
If they don't swap, you get chromosomes carrying just white or just miniature.

So when you cross those double-heterozygous females to wild-type males and look at the male offspring (which only have one X chromosome, so they show whichever mutations are on the X they inherited), you can count how often you see double mutants and neither-mutants (the recombinants) versus single mutants of each kind (the parentals).
The ratio of recombinants to parentals is the map distance — and Sturtevant's insight was that map distance is proportional to physical distance along the chromosome.
Genes near each other recombine rarely.
Genes far apart recombine often.
By counting thousands of fly offspring, you can measure these distances with reasonable precision.
In the fly room, by the early 1920s, this technique had been used to map dozens of genes along all four fly chromosomes.
The map was crude — distances given in "centimorgans," where one centimorgan is roughly 1% recombination frequency — but it was real.
Every fly gene that had a visible phenotype could be placed somewhere on the map.
The fly chromosome started to look less like an abstract concept and more like a physical object with addresses on it.
Then in the 1930s came an extraordinary additional tool: polytene chromosomes.

Fly larvae have salivary glands whose cells contain unusually large chromosomes — the chromosomes have replicated themselves a thousandfold without separating, producing thick banded structures that are visible under a regular light microscope.
Under proper staining, polytene chromosomes show characteristic patterns of dark and light bands — thousands of bands per chromosome, each band corresponding to a specific region of the genome.
So now, when you mapped a gene by recombination to a particular centimorgan position, you could also look at the corresponding band on the polytene chromosome and physically see where it lived.
The genetic map and the cytological map of the chromosome merged.
This was, in 1930s terms, a complete physical and genetic map of a complete animal genome.
Nothing else in biology came close.

The fly had become the test-tube animal — a system in which you could move from a phenotype to a gene to a chromosomal position to a physical band on the chromosome, all using techniques available to a graduate student with a microscope and a few thousand flies.

The big idea to leave this segment with: the fly eye-color genes were not just biology — they were the experimental scaffolding that let classical genetics become a quantitative, physically grounded discipline.
Every concept of linkage, recombination, map distance, and chromosomal location was worked out using flies whose primary visible trait was the color of their eyes.
The work generalized: those mapping concepts apply to humans, mice, plants, fungi — every organism with chromosomes.

Modern human genome-wide association studies use exactly the same recombination logic, scaled to millions of markers and billions of base pairs, that Sturtevant worked out with eye-color crosses in 1913.
Now let's fast-forward to the molecular era and the techniques that turned the fly from a classical-genetics organism into a fully modern molecular-biology platform.
